title: Semaglutide API price plunges amid GLP-1 rush
summary: Global demand for weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy has caused semaglutide prices to drop significantly. Manufacturing has expanded rapidly, and patents are expiring in key markets. Indian drugmakers are launching generics at lower prices. Experts predict...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Synopsis

Global demand for weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy has caused semaglutide prices to drop significantly. Manufacturing has expanded rapidly, and patents are expiring in key markets. Indian drugmakers are launching generics at lower prices. Experts predict further price softening in the coming months, benefiting consumers with wider access to these therapies.

Listen to this article in summarized format

Mumbai: Surging global demand for Novo Nordisk's blockbuster weight-loss and diabetes brands Ozempic and Wegovy, and their generic substitutes, has triggered a price correction in its key ingredient, semaglutide, even as broader pharmaceutical inputs face rising cost pressure amid continued global geopolitical turmoil.

Industry experts told ET that synthetic semaglutide prices have plummeted to $90-$160 per gram, from $900 per gram three years ago, while recombinant variants have fallen to nearly $50 per gram.

Also Read | Dr Reddy's gets nod to roll out generic oral semaglutide drug

The sharp decline is fuelled by a rapid expansion in manufacturing, widening supplier base, and expiry of patents in key markets, including India. Prices are expected to soften a further 20-30% in the coming months, experts said.

"Rapid surge in the global demand for weight-loss drug semaglutide has resulted in a significant drop in the prices of the semaglutide API that is mainly imported from China," said a top industry executive.

"We expect the price to drop further by roughly 20% to 30% in the next few months as last month semaglutide has gone off-patent in several big markets resulting in benefits of volume for suppliers," the executive said.

Fall in synthetic semaglutide prices to $90-$160 per gram may lead to increased affordability and wider access of weight-loss therapies

Danish innovator Novo Nordisk's patent for semaglutide expired on March 20. Consequently, a raft of Indian drugmakers including Sun Pharmaceuticals, Torrent, Dr Reddy's Laboratories, Natco, Eris, and Zydus rolled out generics GLP1s at sharply lower prices than the innovator brands.

For consumers, this could lead to improved affordability and wider access to GLP-1 therapies. However, the final price benefit may be partly offset by rising solvent and input costs linked to global supply disruptions.

Also Read | Mounjaro sales dip amid price war as semaglutide generics gain ground

"The steep drop in semaglutide API prices is largely driven by massive capacity build-up in China, where multi-tonne scale production has significantly outpaced demand growth," said Vishal Manchanda, pharma analyst at Systematix Group.

"As with most APIs, semaglutide is following a classic trajectory-once volumes scale up, manufacturers optimise costs, and prices decline, with profitability shifting towards scale rather than margins," he said.

China's Hangzhou-based Sinopep-Allsino Biopharmaceuticals, a prominent API manufacturer, is the only DCGI-registered supplier so far and is one of the major suppliers of semaglutide API to some of the large Indian companies. Indian companies such as MSN are also expanding capacity, while Apothecon and other entrants are expected to follow in the coming months as Indian generics manufacturers ramp up GLP-1 capacity.

"Both synthetic and recombinant semaglutide API prices have dipped to $50-60 per gram," an industry executive said, requesting anonymity. "The decline in semaglutide API prices isn't just about oversupply. Chinese manufacturers have achieved significant cost efficiencies at scale, allowing them to profit even at much lower price levels."
